messages.content:
You are an expert LaTeX-to-Typst converter.

Convert the supplied complete LaTeX document into one complete, standalone Typst
0.14.2 document. Preserve every visible piece of content and the original
document structure as closely as Typst permits. Do not summarize, omit, or
invent visible content.

Requirements, in priority order:

1. The result must compile with `typst compile` using Typst 0.14.2.
2. Translate the visible document body. LaTeX preamble definitions, macro
   implementations, lengths, counters, and formatting helpers are instructions,
   not visible text; reproduce their visual effect without printing their
   implementation tokens such as `#1` or `#2`.
3. Preserve all visible text, mathematics, headings, lists, tables, figures,
   captions, references, citations, footnotes, and page-level structure.
4. Prefer simple native Typst structure. Do not create helper functions or deep
   nesting when headings, lists, tables, grids, blocks, and figures suffice.
5. Produce a self-contained document. Do not read local files, use `#include`,
   or depend on images or bibliography files that were not supplied.
6. Use no Typst packages unless conversion is otherwise impossible. Never
   invent package names or versions.
7. Omit an explicit font setting unless needed. If needed, use only
   `New Computer Modern`.
8. Return only raw Typst source. Do not use Markdown fences and do not explain
   the conversion.

Typst syntax checks:

- Put all mathematics inside `$...$`. Typst math is not LaTeX math: never copy
  backslash macros, `^`, `_`, `&`, or equation row separators into markup or
  code outside math.
- Use native Typst math forms. Use `hat(x)` for a hat accent. Do not use
  `widehat`, `stack`, or generic symbol modifiers. For multiple equation rows,
  use one math block with `&` alignment points and `\` row breaks.
- In math, quote ordinary words. Separate multi-letter identifiers into valid
  symbols or represent them as quoted upright text instead of guessing names.
- Build tables with comma-separated bracketed cells. Do not carry LaTeX `&`,
  `\\`, `^`, or column specifications into `table()`.
- For algorithms and pseudocode, use one simple block or figure containing
  plain numbered or indented lines. Avoid custom helper functions and deeply
  nested content blocks. Do not write `//` comments inside a content block;
  render comments as ordinary text.
- `#` enters code from markup. Inside an existing code block, use `let`, `if`,
  and `for` without another `#`. Balance every `()`, `[]`, and `{}` delimiter.
- Quote paper names, font names, strings, URLs, and other string values.
- A bare `@name` is a Typst reference. Use it only when a matching label is
  attached to a real target in this document. Enable heading/equation numbering
  before referencing those elements. If a citation target is unavailable,
  render the key visibly as `[key]` instead of creating a dangling reference.
- Use current native symbols and functions. Avoid deprecated angle symbols.
  When uncertain, choose a simpler representation that compiles instead of
  guessing an API.

Convert this complete LaTeX document to Typst:

\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage[margin=1in]{geometry}
\usepackage{array,booktabs,makecell,multirow,tabularx,longtable,colortbl,xcolor,graphicx}
\usepackage{arydshln}
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
\title{Simple Tables}
\author{Source-backed grouped table sample}
\date{}
\begin{document}
\maketitle

42 & main\_id &    & SIMBAD main identifier \\
43 & main\_type &    & SIMBAD main object type \\
44 & sp\_type &    &  SIMBAD spectral classification \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
}
\end{table}
